title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about work and career choices. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Work We Choose
content.subtitle: Rethinking the advice we give young people about careers
content.text:
The advice most commonly given to young people choosing a career — 'follow your passion' — has attracted growing criticism from researchers and career counsellors in recent years. [31]Cal Newport, a computer science professor who has written extensively on the topic, argues that passion is more often the result of mastery than its precondition: we tend to develop passionate engagement with activities as we become genuinely skilled at them, not the other way round.[/31]

This reframing has practical consequences. If passion follows competence, then career satisfaction is less a matter of finding the right fit and more a matter of developing the right skills in whatever field you find yourself. [32]Newport's research suggests that what workers value most — autonomy, creative challenge, and a sense of meaningful contribution — are not qualities inherent to particular jobs but qualities that skilled, experienced workers are able to extract from almost any job.[/32]

The picture is complicated by the structural realities of the labour market. [33]Access to meaningful work is not equally distributed. Those with more education, family connections, and financial resources are better positioned to pursue roles that offer genuine autonomy and development, while those without these advantages often find themselves in work that offers little scope for either.[/33] The 'follow your passion' framework, critics note, is most coherent as advice for those who already have considerable privilege.

A different approach to career choice is offered by the effective altruism movement, which argues that career decisions should be guided not by personal satisfaction but by impact. [34]The organisation 80,000 Hours, named after the approximate number of working hours in a career, advises people to consider which careers would allow them to do the most good in the world, rather than which careers would make them personally happiest.[/34]

This utilitarian model is not without its critics. [35]Some psychologists argue that careers pursued primarily for external reasons — even admirable ones — are less likely to sustain motivation over the long term, and that the psychological benefits of intrinsic motivation, doing something because it is inherently rewarding, should not be discounted in the calculation.[/35]

[36]What emerges from these competing frameworks is not a clear answer but a set of useful questions: What am I good at? What do others need? What can I sustain over time? Career satisfaction, the research suggests, is most likely to be found by those who are honest enough to distinguish between what they romanticise and what they can actually do well.[/36]

questions.question: What is Cal Newport's main argument about career passion?
options: A) That passion is an essential quality that all successful workers must develop before starting.
B) That passion typically develops from becoming skilled at something rather than preceding it.
C) That people who follow their passion are less likely to succeed than those who are pragmatic.
D) That passion alone, without the right qualifications, is unlikely to lead to a good career.
questions.explanation: Newport argues that 'passion is more often the result of mastery than its precondition,' meaning skill comes first and passion follows from it.

questions.question: According to Newport's research, what do workers value most in their jobs?
options: A) High salaries and opportunities for rapid financial advancement.
B) Job security and long-term stability within a single organisation.
C) The ability to work independently and feel that their work makes a meaningful contribution.
D) Good relationships with colleagues and a supportive working environment.
questions.explanation: Workers value most 'autonomy, creative challenge, and a sense of meaningful contribution,' which skilled workers can find in almost any job.

questions.question: What structural problem does the article highlight with 'follow your passion' advice?
options: A) The opportunities for autonomous, fulfilling work are not available to everyone equally.
B) Young people are no longer receiving adequate career guidance in schools.
C) Passionate workers are less reliable and more likely to change jobs frequently.
D) The education system fails to help students identify their genuine interests.
questions.explanation: The text notes that those 'without advantages often find themselves in work that offers little scope' for autonomy, meaning the advice favours the already privileged.

questions.question: What is the main principle behind the career advice offered by 80,000 Hours?
options: A) That people should change careers several times to develop a range of valuable skills.
B) That financial security should be the primary consideration when choosing a career.
C) That early career choices determine a person's potential for the rest of their working life.
D) That career decisions should be made primarily on the basis of how much good one can do.
questions.explanation: 80,000 Hours advises people to 'consider which careers would allow them to do the most good in the world,' prioritising impact over personal happiness.

questions.question: What concern do some psychologists raise about careers chosen primarily for external reasons?
options: A) That they lead to burnout more quickly than careers chosen for personal interest.
B) That motivation is harder to maintain when rewards come from outside rather than from the work itself.
C) That they make it difficult for people to develop a strong sense of professional identity.
D) That external motivations are less reliable than personal values in guiding long-term decisions.
questions.explanation: Psychologists argue that externally driven careers 'are less likely to sustain motivation over the long term,' contrasting with the benefits of intrinsic motivation.

questions.question: What conclusion does the final paragraph draw about career satisfaction?
options: A) That it is impossible to achieve unless one's career aligns perfectly with one's personal values.
B) That it is most reliably achieved by focusing on financial stability during the early career stages.
C) That it is more likely when people are realistic about the gap between what they romanticise and what they do well.
D) That it requires a willingness to change careers at least once during a working life.
questions.explanation: The text concludes that satisfaction is most likely for those 'honest enough to distinguish between what they romanticise and what they can actually do well.'
